This Case Study Digest has been prepared by the faculty of the Board of Studies
(Academic). The objective of this digest is to provide case studies for practice to the
students to enable them to strengthen their preparation in the subject. In case students
need any clarifications or have any suggestions to make for further improvement of the
material contained  herein, they may  write  to  the  Joint  Director, Board  of Studies
(Academic).
All care has been taken to provide interpretations and analysis in a manner useful for the
students. However, the digest has not been specifically discussed by the Council of the
Institute or any of its Committees and the views expressed herein may not be taken to
necessarily represent the views of the Council or any of its Committees.
Permission of the Institute is essential for reproduction of any portion of this book.

====================== PAGE 4 ======================


PREFACE


Business decisions—whether operational or strategic − are guided by an organisation’s long-
term vision and objectives. Effective decisions result from integrated thinking that connects
finance, strategy, compliance, ethics, taxation, and technology. In today’s interconnected
environment, decisions in one area often have cascading effects across multiple functions. The
Integrated Business Solutions (IBS) paper at the CA Final level is structured to mirror this reality.
At its core lies the Case Study− a problem-solving structure designed to develop the ability to
synthesize knowledge across domains. Each case presents a business situation complete with
industry context, stakeholder interests, financial data, and regulatory considerations. Students
are expected to assess the situation holistically and recommend decisions that are not only
compliant and ethical but also strategically sound.
Volume  II includes case studies from diverse sectors such as Film, Fast Fashion, Airlines,
Petrochemicals, Hotels, Online Gaming, Online Food Delivery, and Technology etc. Many of
these  are  sculpted  after  real-life companies,  several  of which are  listed and  operate
internationally.
The Case Studies include both Descriptive Questions and Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQs).
In Descriptive Questions, emphasis is placed on higher-order thinking. Most questions are
structured around verbs such as analyse,  interpret,  advise, recommend, and  evaluate,
encouraging  critical thinking and decision-making. In MCQs, diverse formats are used to
enhance   practical  exam  readiness.  These  include   multiple-correct   options,  most
likely/appropriate options, and "not correct" type MCQs, offering deeper practice and reinforcing
conceptual clarity.
Each case  is supported with comprehensive  solutions, often exploring multiple possible
approaches and outcomes, allowing flexibility in thought and adaptability in application.
Enhanced with industry-specific visuals, conceptual diagrams, and structured layout, the Digest
is designed to provide a practical, boardroom-level experience. Every case encourages students
to assume the role of strategic decision-makers—such as CFOs, Finance Directors, Auditors,
or CEOs − who must balance strategic goals with compliance and ethical conduct.
This volume aims to reinforce the capabilities essential to delivering integrated business
solutions, aligned with the evolving role of Chartered Accountants as strategic partners in
governance, performance, and sustainable value creation.


                          Wishing you happy reading!

CASE STUDY 1


                     Dakshin Fertilizers and Petrochem Ltd.
Dakshin  Fertilizers and Petrochem  Ltd. (DFPL), a leading producer  of  industrial
chemicals, crop nutrition, and mining chemicals, stands as a testament to the vision and
entrepreneurial spirit of its founder, Mr. N. Venkata Ravi. Born in October 1948 in the
modest village of Nungambakkam, Tirupati, Venkata Ravi displayed a natural acumen for
business from an early age. At just 18, he began his career in trade, demonstrating an
insatiable curiosity and a drive to build something extraordinary. In 1962-64, he established
Dakshin Trading Co., a venture through which he quickly gained prominence, earning the
nickname “The Chemical Architect” for his expertise and influence in the chemical market.
Sustained  by  the  success  of  Dakshin  Trading  Co.,  Mr.  Ravi  expanded  into
manufacturing, promoting Dakshin Chemicals Private  Limited  in  1975.  Under  his
leadership, the company achieved profitability within three years, reinforcing his reputation
as a visionary in the chemicals industry. Building on this success, he founded Dakshin
Fertilizers and Petrochem Ltd. (DFPL) in 1980, with an aim to meet the increasing
demand  for  industrial and  agricultural chemicals across  India. Headquartered  in
Thiruvananthapuram,  Kerala,  DFPL’s  main  manufacturing   facility  is  in  Eloor,
Ernakulam, along with additional plants in Dahej (Gujarat), Hisar (Haryana), and
Brahmapur (Odisha). These facilities produce specialty products that serve critical sectors of
the Indian economy, including agriculture, pharmaceuticals, mining, and infrastructure. The
units in Hisar and Brahmapur are currently small-scale units. The Board of DFPL decided
to set up these units in order to have a pan India presence. In the coming years, DFPL
plans to deepen its market presence in northern and eastern India. As and when this is
achieved, the scale of production at these units will be ramped up to cater for the
increased demand. The Board felt that this ramping up of capacity will be easier if there is
already an existing manufacturing set up in these areas.
Venkata Ravi’s long-term aspirations continue to guide DFPL’s business strategy and
operations. The company is committed to leadership  in chemicals and  agri-nutrients,
underpinned by a dedication to excellence and sustainability. DFPL’s vision to become
a leader in chemicals and agri-nutrients, reflects its emphasis on sustainable growth,
aligning closely with India’s rapid development  in  key  sectors  like  agriculture  and
infrastructure. With a mission to manufacture and deliver sustainable chemicals and
agri-nutrients, DFPL aims to be the trusted partner for industries that drive India’s growth.
Through these foundational values, the company continually strives for profitable growth
while remaining committed to a sustainable future, honoring Venkata Ravi’s legacy   of
foresight, determination, and excellence.


====================== PAGE 9 ======================


1.2                  INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

At  a  recent  board  meeting  convened  to  discuss  the  company’s  strategic  direction,
Mr. Venkata Ravi, the Chairman of DFPL, addressed the board members, sharing his
insights on the challenges and opportunities faced by the company.
    “The financial years 2023-24 and 2024-25 have been among the most volatile on record. All our
    raw material inputs saw an unprecedented hike due to global uncertainties, forcing us to raise
     the price of all our finished products. Fortunately, despite these price hikes to off set the increase
      in raw material costs, there has been no impact on the demand for our products from any of our
     core sectors and markets. The robust domestic demand, despite high prices, is driven by the
     growth of sectors that are critical to the Indian economic growth story. Our revenues have
    touched `12,000 crores for FY 2024-25, and profits are expected to increase by 60% compared
      to the previous year. The final results will soon be made available to you and our statutory
     auditors, FRP & Co. We can look forward to strong results and hopefully propose a good dividend
     payout to our shareholders.
     At the same time, volatility requires us to remain agile and resilient. Ammonia, a nitrogen and
    hydrogen compound, is a key ingredient and critical raw material for each of our business
    segments. The availability of ammonia is not a constraint, as we import it primarily from Middle
     Eastern countries. However, due to various factors, including global geopolitical uncertainties,
     the price of this commodity has fluctuated drastically, from ~ US$ 150 per MT to as much as ~
    US$ 750 per MT on an FOB Middle East basis. Adding to the complexity is the challenge of
     transporting the raw material from Kochi Port in Ernakulam to our manufacturing facility in Eloor,
    on the outskirts of Ernakulam. Out of our total requirement of 1,600 Metric Tons Per Day (MTPD),
    we meet almost 80% of  it through imports, requiring the safe movement of ~ 65,000 trucks
     annually to Eloor.
    Given the operational and  logistical challenges,  I propose  setting up our own ammonia
     manufacturing plant with a capacity of 1,500 MTPD at Eloor, within our current manufacturing
     complex. Our Eloor facility already has a well-established natural gas pipeline, which is a key
    component in the production of ammonia. The piped ammonia from the new plant can be supplied
      directly to our finished goods manufacturing units within the same complex. Having our own
    ammonia manufacturing unit will make us more resilient and mitigate risks associated with the
     supply of this key raw material. Our forex savings due to import substitution could potentially
     reach ` 17,000 crores (~US$ 2.0 Bn) over the next 10 years. Direct supply to our finished
     products manufacturing facility will also help reduce storage, transportation, and logistics costs.
     Freight cost savings alone could amount to ~` 4,500 per ton. Our goals for creating an
     environmentally sustainable business will also be effectively supported by this project.
     Currently, none of our competitors have the capability to set up such a large ammonia
     manufacturing plant within India. Therefore, in times of supply uncertainties,  volatility, and
     increased pressure on margins, it is prudent to commence this project as soon as possible. The
       initial capital expenditure (Capex) is estimated to be around ` 2,000 crores.”
The Board of Directors of the company, through a resolution passed at its meeting, decided
to borrow an additional sum of ` 1,500 crores from the company’s bankers by following the
due procedure as per the Companies Act, 2013. These funds will be used to set up the
proposed ammonia manufacturing unit. Following the Board’s approval for this borrowing,
DFPL approached FRP & Co., the statutory auditors, to have the prospective financial
information examined. This examination will add credibility to the company’s application
when they approach the bankers for the additional loan.


====================== PAGE 10 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                    1.3

Y & E Law Firm, a boutique legal practice based in Ernakulam, has been appointed to
ensure compliance with the necessary regulatory requirements for the project. With an
annual turnover of less than ` 1 crore, the firm has earned a strong reputation for its
expertise in corporate law, regulatory compliance, and dispute resolution. Despite its modest
size, Y & E is known for delivering high-quality, client- focused legal solutions, making it a
trusted choice for complex assignments. The firm successfully completed the required
compliances within 30 days for a consolidated fee of ` 10,00,000.
CA. Fiona, the proprietor of FRP & Co., is uncertain whether she can get involved in the
examination of the prospective financial information to be submitted to DFPL’s bankers
relating to a ` 1,500 crore loan. She seeks the opinion of her peer Chartered Accountant,
CA. T. Ramaswamy, on this matter.
The additional loan of ` 1,500 crores has been sanctioned by the company’s bankers.
Below are some key dates related to the new borrowing of ` 1,500 crores for the project:


           2025          2025          2025          2025


At the end of August 2025, as project construction began, Mr. Ravi shifted his focus to strategic
aspects within the company’s existing business operations. He requested that the heads of the
various manufacturing units—industrial chemicals, crop  nutrition, and mining chemicals—
conduct an in-depth analysis of their business performance. The goal is to identify measures
that will help DFPL establish a sustainable business model. This includes evaluating whether
the selling prices and costs of each product are appropriate to support long-term profitability
and competitiveness.
Mr. Panchal, head of the crop nutrition business, began analyzing his unit’s financial and
environmental data, focusing  particularly on  fertilizer production. The crop  nutrition  unit
manufactures two main types of fertilizers: Grade A and Grade B, priced at ` 560 and ` 800 per
kilogram, respectively. During production, both products pass through three cost centers—CC1,
CC2, and CC3.
The direct material cost per kilogram of fertilizer totals ` 600, divided as follows: ` 180 in CC1,
` 240 in CC2, and ` 180 in CC3. Additionally, direct labour costs amount to ` 400 per kilogram,
allocated as ` 120 to CC1, ` 160 to CC2, and ` 120 to CC3. The cost allocation between Grade
A and Grade B fertilizers varies by cost center: in CC1, 30% of costs are assigned to Grade A


====================== PAGE 11 ======================


1.4                  INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

and 70% to Grade B; in CC2, costs are split equally, with 50% allocated to each grade; and in
CC3, the allocation mirrors CC1, with 30% to Grade A and 70% to Grade B. Additional expenses
of ` 300 per kilogram, considered overheads, are divided equally between Grade A and
Grade B.
Recently, Mr Panchal encountered the concept of environmental management accounting and
recognized  the importance  of  incorporating environmental costs  into  pricing. Given the
oligopolistic nature of the Indian fertilizer market, he saw this as a strategic advantage. His
further analysis revealed that the overheads of ` 300 per kilogram include incinerator costs of
` 180 per kilogram, necessary for disposing of the solid waste generated during production.
Grade A and Grade B have differing environmental impacts: Grade A produces 6 tons of waste
annually (2 tons in CC1, 3 tons in CC2, and 1 ton in CC3), while Grade B generates 9 tons (2
tons in CC1, 2 tons in CC2, and 5 tons in CC3). Mr. Panchal believes it would be appropriate to
adjust the incinerator cost allocation to better reflect each product’s environmental impact, while
the remaining overheads of ` 120 per kilogram will continue to be equally shared between Grade
A and Grade B.
Public perception of fertilizer and petrochemical industry is that  it is a major contributor to
environmental pollution. This is due to the release of harmful chemicals into the environment –
land, water and air. The emission of greenhouse gases is a major contributor to climate change,
which has wreaked havoc globally. Mr. Ravi realizes that the future of business depends on
creating a sustainable and environmentally friendly business model. Further, DFPL is a large
company that supports the livelihood of roughly 5,000 employees. Mr. Ravi wants DFPL to be a
good corporate citizen and has undertaken several CSR initiatives. He has requested the unit
heads of the 3 units to list their division’s CSR activities using which a Triple Bottom Line report
will be prepared. This will be used for the upcoming call with analysts / investors next month to
discuss business performance.
Mr. Panchal, the head of the crop nutrition unit, has undertaken a series of initiatives aligned
with DFPL’s ethical and environmental goals. Firstly, he ensured due diligence within the supply
chain to confirm vendors adhere to ethical labour practices. Upon discovering instances of
unethical practices, including non-payment of wages and hazardous working conditions with two
vendors, he promptly terminated their services and onboarded alternate vendors. In the area of
environmental responsibility, DFPL introduced a state-of-the- art technology this year to mitigate
ammonia emissions, reducing them  to levels  significantly below regulatory requirements,
despite the essential use of liquid ammonia in fertilizer production. Additionally, during the
financial year 2024-25, approximately ` 1,600 crores—accounting for 40% of the turnover from
the crop  nutrition unit—was generated from environmentally  friendly  fertilizers (EFF), a
significant increase from the previous 15-20% share. Moreover, Mr. Panchal has focused on
enhancing  the  internal  reporting mechanism  to  include  non-financial  metrics, such as
innovation, brand  equity, and  competitor  activities,  thereby  integrating  external market
considerations. He has submitted these activities to Mr. Ravi’s secretary for inclusion in DFPL’s
Triple Bottom Line report.

CASE STUDIES                                    1.7


                              Extracts from the Annual Report 2024-25
                          Dakshin Fertilizers and Petrochem Ltd.


Corporate Governance
Philosophy on Corporate Governance
Effective corporate governance practices form the strong foundation on which successful
commercial  enterprises  are  built  to  endure. The Company’s philosophy on corporate
governance involves overseeing business strategies while ensuring fiscal accountability,
ethical corporate behavior, and fairness to all stakeholders, including employees, investors,
customers, regulators, suppliers, and society at large. Strong leadership and effective
corporate governance practices have always been hallmarks of the Company.
Board Of Directors
The composition of the Company’s Board reflects a commitment to  diversity and  is
exemplified by its balanced, independent structure. The Board includes a well-proportioned
representation of Executive, Non-Executive, and Independent Directors, strengthening the
Company’s organizational capabilities. Each Board member has been carefully selected to
provide a valuable blend of expertise, experience, oversight, and security, all aimed at
enhancing the Company’s overall effectiveness.
 Name of Director         Profile

                         Science graduate from the University of Madras, he is renowned
     Venkata Ravi                                for expertise in chemical and petrochemical sectors, particularly

          Director                       enhance product quality. Actively participating in national forums,
                       he promotes best practices and policies that drive innovation,
                             collaboration, and manufacturing excellence, positioning India’s
                         chemical industry as a global leader.

                           growth.

     Lila Poonawalla7                               of Insurance Institute of India (FIII-Life).
   Independent Director


1.      His  initial appointment as an Independent Director was for a term of 1 year and followed by a
       reappointment  for  another  term  of 3  years,  which ended on 30th July 2025. Based on the
       recommendation of the nomination and remuneration committee and after taking into account the
       performance evaluation of his four years and considering the knowledge, acumen, expertise, experience
       and  the  substantial  contribution he  brings   to the Board, the committee has recommended the
       appointment of Ajay to the Board for an additional term of 3 years. If the proposal is accepted as per
        applicable laws and regulations, Mr. Ajay will be 67 years of age by the completion of his third term.

       Ord and Mr. A. Bindra as Independent  Directors of the company for a first term of 3 consecutive years,
         effective 2nd February 2025.

 Dear Salil,
 Gaspro India Limited (GIL) our main supplier of natural gas plans to set up a Liquified Natural Gas
 (LNG) receipt facility at Gopalpur port, Odisha. Our crop nutrition unit in Brahmapur already
 receives natural gas supplies from GIL’s unit in Paradeep, Odisha. Now that GIL plans to set up
  its facility in Gopalpur our ability to scale up operations on the East Coast will become easier. We
 have made significant market penetration for our crop nutrition products in Odisha and Northern
  belt of Andhra Pradesh. I plan to start a bigger unit with a capacity of 10,00,000 MT/ Yr. in Gopalpur.
 This unit  will exclusively manufacture  fertilizers only. Since this  will be our biggest venture in
  fertilizers, the new facility in Gopalpur will be created as a separate entity. It will be company that
  is a joint venture between DFPL and Greenspan Gmbh, a reputed fertilizer company based in
 Germany. Greenspan Gmbh can provide technical assistance to develop energy efficient, modern
 manufacturing techniques. This will enable CYPL to achieve the scale of production that is required
  to get the benefit of cost economies of scale. The profitability of this unit will be driven significantly by
 the cost efficiencies due to economies of scale that we can achieve by operating at large scale.
 The Brahmapur facility will no longer be required and will be shut down. Therefore, I plan to shift
 some of our existing machinery from Brahmapur facility to the proposed facility at Gopalpur. The
 value of the second-hand machinery so transferred will be ` 45 crores, 15% of the total plant and
 machinery required at the new facility. Greenspan Gmbh also plans to transfer some of its plant
 and machinery to the Gopalpur facility. These machineries have the latest technology and have a
 larger processing capacity, much more than what we can build or buy domestically in India.
 However, these are also second hand machineries that was used by a subsidiary of Greenspan
 Gmbh within its facility at Weisbaden, Germany. The new company at Gopalpur will be importing
 these machineries from this German subsidiary valued at ` 120 crores, 40% of the total plant and
 machinery required at the new facility. These machineries have been used only by the German
 subsidiary of Greenspan Gmbh and by no other entity whether in Germany or India. In addition,
 we are in talks to acquire industrial land worth ` 100 crores at Gopalpur. The project planning has
 not yet been finalized; it is in the nascent stage. At the same time, I need some advice from you
 regarding a taxation angle.
  I recall that as per the Income Tax Act, 1961, companies are eligible to claim certain “investment-
 linked tax incentives” for specified businesses. Please advise, from the point of view of the new
 company assessee, the conditions subject to which the above capital expenditures can be
 claimed as deductions.
 Regards,
 Managing Director

                                    Annexure: 2
 Auditor Investigated Following Allegations of Insider Trading, 30th June 2025
 The Securities and Exchange Board of India today initiated an inquiry against CA. Fiona, the
 proprietor of FRP & Co., a reputed Chartered Accountants firm in Mumbai. FRP & Co. is the
 statutory auditor of Dakshin Fertilizers and Petrochem Ltd. (DFPL), a listed company on both BSE
 and NSE (BSE: 500123 | NSE: DFPL | ISIN: INE000A00123). The allegation is that CA. Fiona was
 privy to DFPL’s plans to open an ammonia manufacturing unit within its manufacturing complex at
 Eloor. In March 2025, the company had even approached her services to help it avail loan facilities
 from  its bankers. This project is of vital importance since  it enables the company to build
 substantial agility and robustness in its operations, due to substantial cost advantages as well as
 an unbeatable competitive edge over its competitors. However, as soon as she became privy to
  this information in the month of March 2025, CA. Fiona shared this information with Mr. A. Sahu, a
 stockbroker who trades on both BSE and NSE. At the time of passing, this information was not
 available in the public domain through the company. Following this, Mr. A. Sahu had taken
 positions on DFPL’s shares and when the share price rose in the month of May 2025, he netted a
  profit of approximately ` 3 crore on account of this trade alone. This incident came to light following
 an independent investigation launched by SEBI against Mr. A. Sahu, who was under the scanner
  for irregularities relating to insider trading deals of other companies as well. SEBI has claimed that
   it has evidence against CA. Fiona in the form of emails and telephonic conversations. CA. Fiona
 vehemently denies these charges and has decided to appeal against such charges. She  is
 currently co-operating with the authorities in this regard.

I.     Multiple Choice Questions
1.    With respect to the fresh borrowing of ` 1,500 crore loan for the project (qualifying
      asset), identify the commencement date for capitalization of borrowing cost:
       (a)     1st May, 2025
       (b)     1st June, 2025
        (c)    15th June, 2025
       (d)     1st August, 2025
2.   As a peer Chartered Accountant, what advise will CA. T. Ramaswamy give to
     CA. Fiona-
       (a)   No, CA.  Fiona  should  not  accept  the  assignment.  The  Chartered
           Accountants Act, 1949 states that a Chartered Accountant  in  practice
              shall be deemed of professional misconduct, if he permits his name  or
            the name of the firm to be used in connection with an estimate of earnings
            contingent upon future transactions in a manner which may lead to the
              belief that he vouches for the accuracy of the forecast.
       (b)   Yes, CA.  Fiona can  accept  the  assignment. There is no restriction
            contained  within the Chartered Accountants   Act,  1949   regarding


====================== PAGE 20 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                 1.13

            association  of a Chartered Accountant’s name with prospective financial
             information.
        (c)   Yes, CA. Fiona can accept the assignment. The Chartered Accountants
             Act, 1949 does  not  preclude  a  Chartered Accountant from  being
            associated  with prospective  financial  information. She should  clearly
             indicate in her report, the sources of information, the basis of forecasts and
             also the major assumptions made in arriving at the forecasts. She can then
           vouch for the accuracy of the forecasts that will provide requisite credibility
              to the information.
       (d)   Yes, CA. Fiona can accept the assignment. The Chartered Accountants
             Act, 1949 does  not  preclude  a  Chartered Accountant from  being
            associated with prospective financial information as long as she does not
           vouch for the accuracy of the forecasts. Moreover, she should clearly
             indicate in her report, the sources of information, the basis of forecasts and
            also the major assumptions made in arriving at the forecasts.

6.    With respect to the allegations made against CA. Fiona, you are required to select the
       correct option which indicates whether this is a case of insider trading or not and if it is
      a case of insider trading then the quantum of penalty that can be levied.
       (a)       It is not a case of insider trading since CA. Fiona is the statutory auditor of DFPL
           and is therefore cannot be considered as an insider to the company.
       (b)       It is not a case of insider trading since CA. Fiona did not disclose any price-
              sensitive information.
        (c)       It is a case of insider trading and therefore the penalty leviable would be not less
             than ` 10 lakh, but which may extend to ` 25 crore or three times the profit made
             out of insider trading, whichever is higher.
       (d)       It is a case of insider trading and therefore the penalty leviable would be not less
             than ` 25 crore or three times the profit made out of insider trading, whichever is
              lower.
7.   Can Mr. Ajay be reappointed as an independent director for another term of 3
      years?
       (a)   Under the Companies Act, 2013, an independent director can be appointed for
          a maximum period of 3 years. Mr. Ajay can be reappointed as an independent
               director for the remaining period of the first term, i.e., 2 years and serving
              this balance term construed as part of the earlier incomplete term only.
       (b)   Under the Companies Act, 2013, an independent director can be appointed for
          2 consecutive terms – each term being 5 years (i.e. 10 years). Mr. Ajay can be
            reappointed as an independent director for another term of 3 years since his
                   first two terms were of lesser period instead of 5 years as provided under the
          Companies Act, 2013.


====================== PAGE 23 ======================


1.16                 INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

        (c)   Under the Companies Act, 2013, an independent director can be appointed
              for maximum 2 consecutive terms – each term can be upto 5 years (i.e. 10
              years). Mr. Ajay cannot be reappointed as an independent director since he
                will be 67 years of age on completion of the proposed term of 3 years. Any
            person reaching 65 years   of age cannot be appointed as an independent
               director.
       (d)   Under the Companies Act, 2013, an independent director can be appointed for a
          maximum 2 consecutive terms – each term can be upto 5 years (i.e. 10
             years). Mr. Ajay cannot be reappointed as an independent director since he has
            completed 2 consecutive terms. Appointment for a term less than 5 years is
             treated as one term.

1.18                 INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

            ANSWERS TO THE CASE STUDY 1

I.    Answers to the Multiple Choice Questions
1.     (c)    15th June 2025.
           Reason:
          As per para 17 of Ind AS 23, Borrowing costs, the capitalization of
           borrowing cost on qualifying asset starts when all the three conditions are
          met  i.e. expenditures are  incurred  for  acquiring  or  constructing  the
             qualifying asset, borrowing costs are incurred on those qualifying asset,
          and the activities necessary to prepare the qualifying asset for its intended
           use or sale are undertaken.
         On 15th June 2025, all three conditions necessary for capitalisation of cost
            are satisfied:
                   (i)     Incurring of borrowing cost commenced from 1st May 2025
                    (ii)    Activities necessary to prepare qualifying asset for its intended use
               began from 1st June 2025
                     (iii)   Expenditure began to be incurred on the qualifying asset from 15th
                June 2025.
            Hence, on 15th June, 2025, all the three conditions prescribed  in Ind AS 23 for
         commencement of capitalization of borrowing cost are met. Hence, the
         commencement date would be 15th June, 2025.
2.     (d)   Yes, CA. Fiona can accept the assignment. The Chartered Accountants
             Act, 1949 does not preclude a Chartered Accountant from being associated
              with prospective financial information as long as he does not vouch for the
            accuracy of the forecasts. Moreover, she should clearly indicate in her report,
            the sources of information, the basis of forecasts and also the major
           assumptions made in arriving at the forecasts.
           Reason:
           Clause 3 of the Second Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949
             states that that a chartered accountant in practice shall be deemed to be
              guilty of professional misconduct, if he permits his name or the name of his
             firm to be used in connection with an estimate of earnings contingent upon


====================== PAGE 26 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                 1.19

             future transactions in a manner which may lead to the belief that he
           vouches for the accuracy of the forecast.
          The above clause does not preclude a Chartered accountant  from
            associating his name with prospective financial statements. A chartered
           accountant can participate in the preparation of profit or financial forecasts
          and can review them, provided he indicates clearly in his report the sources
              of information, the basis of forecasts and also the major assumptions made
              in arriving at the forecasts and so long as he does not vouch for the
           accuracy of the forecasts.
3.     (b)        ii and iii.
           Reason:
              In  house  manufacture  of  ammonia  mitigates  risks  and  external
             uncertainties within the supply chain. This makes the supply chain system
            robust and resilient to any external supply shock. Manufacturing ammonia
            also allows DFPL to realize substantial cost savings by reducing forex
           outgo as well as making operations more efficient resulting in savings in
            operational costs like storage, transportation and logistics costs.
           Statement i is incorrect as in-house manufacturing ammonia assists DFPL
              in pursuing a cost leadership strategy since it results in substantial cost
            savings. It does not help in differentiating DFPL’s products with that of its
               rivals.
           Statement iv is incorrect since in house manufacturing of a critical raw
             material  like ammonia would be an example  of backward  vertical
             integration rather than a horizontal integration within the supply chain.
4.     (a)      i, ii and iii.
           Reason:
           Statements i,  ii and  iii are the potential hurdles that Mr. Panchal can face
            while carrying out  this exercise. Statement  iv  is  incorrect.  Typically,
            decisions based on non-financial measures have a long-term focus. Rather
              this ensures the long-term profitability and business sustainability of an
             organization.


====================== PAGE 27 ======================


1.20                 INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

5.     (a)       i – c, ii – a, iii – d, iv – b.
           Reason:
                   (i)   Due  diligence  of  vendors  within  the  supply  chain  to  ensure
                 compliance of ethical business labour practices – People
                    (ii)   Development of state of  art technology to reduce emissions of
              ammonia to negligible levels much below the regulatory requirements
                – Planet
                    (iii)   Increasing  contribution  of  Environmentally  Friendly  Fertilizers
                 (EFFs) to the total turnover of the crop nutrition unit - Profit
                (iv)   Improving internal reporting mechanism to include non-financial
                 parameters  to  include  external market considerations  like new
                   innovations, brand  equity and  competitors’ movements –  Not
                   relevant
6.     (c)      It is a case of insider trading and therefore the penalty leviable would be not
              less than ` 10 lacs, but which may extend to ` 25 crore or three times the
               profit made out of insider trading, whichever is higher.
           Reason:
             Section 15G of the SEBI Act, 1992. Information passed on by CA. Fiona to Mr.
            A. Sahu regarding the expansion of DFPL  to build  its own ammonia
           manufacturing plant, a  critical raw material  for  its operations,  will be
           considered as unpublished price sensitive information and CA. Fiona will
          be  considered as  insider as  per  Regulation  2(1)(g)  of SEBI  (PIT)
            Regulations, 2015.
7.     (d)   Under the Companies Act, 2013, an independent director can be appointed
               for a maximum of 2 consecutive terms – each term can be upto 5 years (i.e.
           10 years). Mr. Ajay cannot be reappointed as an independent director since
           he has completed 2 consecutive terms. Appointment for a term less than 5
           years is treated as one term.
           Reason:
        MCA has clarified that an independent director can be appointed for a term
              of less than 5 years. However, any appointment whether of 5 or less than
          5 years will be regarded as ‘one term’. Section 149 (11) of the Companies
             Act, 2013  clearly  stipulates  that no  person  can  hold office as an
           independent  director  for more than  ‘two consecutive  terms’.  Thus,


====================== PAGE 28 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                 1.21

             irrespective of the duration of each of the two terms (whether the same
           aggregates to 10 years or less) a person holding office for two consecutive
           terms shall be eligible  for re-appointment only after the expiry of three
           years of ceasing to become an independent director.
8.     (d)       i, ii, iv, v.
           Reason:
           Under Section 197 of the Companies Act, 2013, a company is allowed to pay
           remuneration  to  its  Non-Executive  Directors (NEDs)  for  services
           rendered if:
               (a)   The services provided are of a professional nature and
               (b)   The Nomination and Remuneration Committee determines that the
                    Director possesses  the necessary  qualifications  to  practice  the
                    profession.
           Thus, statements  i and  ii appear to be correct, as the professional nature
              of the services and the qualification of the Director are prerequisites for such
           payments which is also justified by his profile.
             Additionally,  all Related Party Transactions (RPTs) and any material
            modifications require prior approval of the Audit Committee of the listed
               entity, as specified under Regulation 23(2) of the SEBI (LODR) Regulations,
            2015. Only those members of the Audit Committee who are independent
             directors are permitted to approve such transactions.
           Thus, statements iv and v are also correct, as Audit Committee approval
               is required, and the approval must come from independent directors.
           However, statement iii is incorrect. Even if the transaction does not exceed
          one crore rupees,  it  still qualifies as a Related Party Transaction and,
              therefore, requires Audit Committee approval, regardless  of the amount
             involved.
              In summary, the company’s transaction is conducted in  the ordinary
          course of business and on an arm’s length basis, which eliminates the need
                for Board approval under Section 188. However, all related party transactions
           must be approved by the Audit Committee as per the regulations, making
           Option (d) the correct choice.
9.     (b)   No, her appointment  is invalid as  an independent director because the
             receipts of the Y & E exceed 10% of its gross turnover.


====================== PAGE 29 ======================


1.22                 INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

           Reason:
           According to Section 149(6) of the Companies Act, 2013, in relation to a
          company, an independent  director means a  director  (other  than a
          managing director or a whole-time director or a nominee director), and who
             neither himself nor any of his relatives is or has been an employee or
             proprietor or a partner,  in any  of the 3  financial years immediately
           preceding the financial year in which he is proposed to be appointed, of—
         a firm of auditors or company secretaries in practice or cost auditors
                    of the company or its holding, subsidiary or associate company; or
         any legal or a consulting firm that has or had any transaction with the
                 company, its holding, subsidiary or associate company amounting to
             10% or more of the gross turnover of such firm.
            Ms. Aditi is a partner in Y & E Law Firm, which has an annual turnover of less than
          ` 1 crore. However, the fee of ` 10,00,000 received from the company represents
           more than 10% of the firm’s gross turnover. As a result, her appointment as
          an  independent  director  is  invalid because  the  law  prohibits such
           appointments if the transaction exceeds the specified threshold of 10%.

            Part A: Allocation of Incinerator Cost (based on waste produced at each
            cost centre)
                Particulars                  CC1     CC2     CC3       Total
             Grade A                       24        36        12        72
             Grade B                       24        24        60       108
                 Total Incinerator Cost            48        60        72       180
             Part B: Allocation of Other Overhead Cost (allocated equally to each
            product)
                Particulars                  CC1     CC2     CC3       Total
             Grade A                       20        20        20        60
             Grade B                       20        20        20        60
                 Total Other Overhead Cost        40        40        40       120

             Part C: Total Overhead Cost
                Particulars                   CC1     CC2     CC3      Total
             Grade A                        44       56       32       132
             Grade B                        44       44       80       168
                Total Overhead Cost              88       100      112      300
            Using the activity-based cost allocation methodology, profit earned per kg of
           Grade A and Grade B fertilizer is ` 48 per kg and ` 12 per kg respectively. The
                profitability as a % of selling price per kg works out Grade A: 8.57% and Grade
             B: 1.50%.
         (iii)   Activity based cost allocation methodology allocates the incinerator cost on
          a more logical basis rather than an ad-hoc distribution as per the original cost
              allocation method. It can be seen from Table, that out of the 15 tons of annual
                total waste generated, Grade A generates 6 tons annually, whereas Grade B


====================== PAGE 32 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                 1.25

      generates 9 tons annually. Hence, it would be appropriate to allocate the
       incinerator cost more to Grade B since it generates more waste. This is done
     under the activity-based cost allocation method.
      Accordingly, the profitability of Grade A increases  from ` 30 per kg as per
       original cost allocation method to ` 48 per kg as per the activity-based cost
       allocation methodology. This  is an increase of ` 18 per kg. At the same
       time, the profitability of Grade B fertilizer drops by ` 18 per kg when the
       allocation method is changed from  original  cost  allocation method (` 30
      per kg) to activity-based-cost allocation method (` 12 per kg). The summary
       of impact on product profitability on account of change in method can be
     summarized as:
        Product            Profit in ` per kg of                      Profit %
                                   fertilizer produced
                          Original      ABC          Original       ABC
                   method       as per       method        as per
                         EMA method             EMA method
       Grade A         30           48          5.36%         8.57%
       Grade B         30           12          3.75%         1.50%
     By allocating the incinerator cost on a more appropriate basis, DFPL could arrive
       at a more accurate profitability for each product.
(iv)   In the given case, Mr. Panchal’s opinion that  it would be appropriate to
      revise the incinerator cost allocation since each product has a different impact
     on the environment is correct. The benefits of using such an activity-based-
       costing methodology over the traditional cost allocation method is that:
       (a)   The company was able to conclude that Grade B product generated
          more  waste  than  Grade A product resulting in more accurate
            product costing.
       (b)   Based on the activity cost allocation methodology, subject to prevailing
            market conditions, company can increase the market price of Grade B
                fertilizer by 18 per kg fertilizer, to recoup the extra overhead  cost
              allocation.  If found unviable the company can even, consider phasing
             out production of Grade B.
        (c)   The company can explore the possibility of adopting more sustainable
           manufacturing processes that reduces waste generated, this would
            increase its overall profitability.
       (d)    In addition to the above benefits, adopting activity-based costing
           methodology  makes  the  company  environmentally   friendly  by
              highlighting the need to reduce its carbon footprint.


====================== PAGE 33 ======================


1.26                 INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

11.  As per Section 180(1)(c) of the Company’s Act, 2013, the Board of Directors
      without obtaining the approval of the shareholders in a general meeting through
       special resolution, can borrow funds including funds already borrowed upto an
     amount which does not exceed the aggregate of the paid- up share capital, free
      reserves and securities premium. Such borrowing shall not include temporary
      loans obtained from the company’s bankers in the ordinary course of business. Free
      reserves do not include reserves set apart for specific purposes.
      According to the above provisions, the Board of Directors of DFPL can borrow,
      without obtaining the approval of the shareholders in a general meeting, upto the
     amount calculated as below:
         Particulars                                                                      (In crores)
        Paid up share capital                                        ` 2,000
        General Reserves (being free reserve)                            ` 500
       Debenture Redemption Reserve (not considered as this is a specific                -
         reserve kept apart for specific purpose of debenture redemption)
        Provision for Taxation (not considered as this is a specific reserve                  -
        kept apart for taxation)
         Securities Premium                                          ` 500
        Aggregate  of paid-up share  capital,  free reserves and           ` 3,000
         securities premium
         Total borrowing power of the Board of Directors (100% of aggregate     ` 3,000
         paid-up share capital, free reserves and securities premium) – from
         step above
        Less: Amount already borrowed
       Long Term Loan                                           ` 2,000
        Short term creditors (not considered as it is a temporary financing                -
        arrangement in the ordinary course of business)
      Amount that the Board of Directors can borrow without the     ` 1,000
        approval of shareholders in a general meeting
       In the present case, the directors of DFPL by a resolution passed at its meeting
     have decided to borrow an additional sum of ` 1,500 crores. Apparently, the
     proposed borrowing will be beyond the powers of the Board of Directors.
     The management of DFPL therefore should take steps to convene the general
      meeting and pass a special resolution by the members in the meeting as stated
       in Section 180 (1)(c) of the Companies Act, 2013. Then only, the proposed borrowing
       of ` 1,500 crores will be valid and binding on the company and its members.


====================== PAGE 34 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                 1.27

12.   DFPL has an expected dividend per share of ` 55, growing at an annual rate of 5%,
       with a required rate of return of 10%.
      Using the Gordon Growth Model (P = D1 / (r - g)), the estimated intrinsic value of DFPL’s
      stock is calculated as follows:
     P = D1 / (r - g) = 55/ 10% -5% = `1,100
      This intrinsic value of `1,100 aligns with DFPL’s actual market price in March
      2025, as shown in the chart extracted from the annual report. DFPL’s share price
      trends around ` 1,100 in March 2025, which is consistent with its calculated intrinsic
      value.
      This suggests that the market perceives DFPL’s growth prospects being fairly
      represented in  its current valuation. The required rate of return (10%) and
      expected dividend growth rate (5%) are viewed as appropriate reflections of
      DFPL’s value. This implies that investors see DFPL’s future earnings and growth
       potential as stable, supporting the current market price.
13.   Section 35AD of the Income-tax Act, 1961 provides for investment linked tax
       incentives. It allows for 100% deduction of the capital expenditure incurred during the
      previous year, wholly and exclusively for setting up specified business, of which
        fertilizer producers is one of them. However, expenditure incurred on acquisition
       of any land, goodwill or financial instrument would not be eligible for deduction.
     The new company that is a joint venture at Gopalpur port, Odisha will exclusively
     produce fertilizers. Hence, capital expenditures subject to certain conditions can
     be claimed as deduction under section 35AD. However, expenditure of ` 100
      crore towards acquisition of land at Gopalpur cannot be claimed as deduction
     under section 35AD.
     One of the conditions subject to which deduction can be allowed under this
      section is regarding transfer of previously used plant or machinery.
     The new joint venture should not be set up by the transfer of machinery  or  plant
       previously used for any purpose. In order to satisfy this condition, the total value of
       plant or machinery so transferred should not exceed 20% of the total plant or
     machinery used in the specified business.
      For the purpose of this condition, machinery or plant  will not  be considered as
      previously used if it had been used outside India by any other person other than
      the assessee provided the following conditions are satisfied:
       (a)   such plant or machinery was not used in India at any time prior to the date
              of its installation by the assessee
       (b)    the plant or machinery was imported to India from a foreign country


====================== PAGE 35 ======================


1.28                 INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

        (c)   no deduction in respect of depreciation of such plant or machinery has been
            allowed to any person at any time prior to the date of installation by the
           assessee
     Working back, the estimated total investment in plant and machinery works out to
     be ` 300 crores (` 120 crores/40%).
     Given that machinery currently being used by the Brahmapur unit of DFPL will
     be  transferred  to  this new company proposed in Gopalpur. Valued at ` 45
      crores, it will comprise of 15% of the total plant and machinery required at the
     new facility. In addition, certain machinery will be imported from the subsidiary
       of Greenspan Gmbh, Weisbaden, Germany. Valued at ` 120 crores,  it  will
      comprise 40% of the total plant and machinery required at the new facility. Hence,
      the total machinery that the proposed unit will have, which have been previously
     used for other purpose will be 55% of its total plant and machinery. However,
      the imported machinery from the German subsidiary of Greenspan Gmbh has not
     been used in India at any time prior to the date  of  its  installation by  the
      assessee. Also being thus far solely owned by the subsidiary in Germany, it can
     be concluded  that no deduction  in respect  of depreciation of such plant or
     machinery has been allowed to any  person at any time prior to the date of
       installation by the assessee. Hence,  if these conditions hold until the date of
      actual installation of these imported machinery at the Gopalpur facility, they
        will  not be  treated as  being  previously  used   for  any  other  purpose.
      Consequently,  it is only the transfer of machinery valued at ` 45 crores, from
     Brahmapur unit of DFPL to the new company that comprises of 15% of the total
      plant or machinery that will be considered as previously used for other purpose.
      This is within the overall limit of 20% of such machinery as restricted by section
     35AD.
       Please note that details of other capital expenditures, for the rest of the plant
      and machinery worth `135 crores, will be required to be considered eligible for
       “investment linked tax incentives” under Section 35AD
     Hence, based on the information provided, the new company can currently
      claim these capital expenditures of ` 165 crores for machinery as “investment
      linked tax incentives” and can claim deduction under Section 35AD.


====================== PAGE 36 ======================


CASE STUDY 2


                           Crop Yield Private Limited
Dakshin Fertilizers and Petrochem Ltd. (DFPL)  is a reputed company manufacturing
industrial  chemicals,  crop   nutrition  and  mining  chemicals.  Headquartered   in
Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, DFPL’s main manufacturing facility is in Eloor, Ernakulam,
along with additional plants in Dahej (Gujarat), Hisar (Haryana), and Brahmapur (Odisha).
These facilities produce specialty products that serve critical sectors of the Indian economy,
including agriculture, pharmaceuticals, mining, and infrastructure. The units in Hisar and
Brahmapur are currently small-scale units. The Board of DFPL decided to set up these units
in order to have a pan India presence. In the coming years, DFPL plans to deepen its market
presence  in northern and eastern India. As and when  this is achieved, the scale of
production at these units will be ramped up to cater for the increased demand. The Board felt
that this ramping up of capacity will be easier if there is already an existing manufacturing
set up in these areas.
Setting up new manufacturing facility at Gopalpur and closure of Brahmapur facility
DFPL has established a strong foothold in the crop nutrition market, including fertilizers,
with significant penetration in Odisha and the northern belt of Andhra Pradesh. To capitalize
on rising demand in these markets, the company  is poised to expand  its production
capacity, paving the way for increased market presence.
The fertilizer industry operates under stringent regulations and is supported by government
subsidies aimed at ensuring affordability for farmers. In such a regulated environment, achieving
cost efficiencies is crucial for enhancing profitability. As a capital-intensive sector, the fertilizer
industry depends heavily on economies of scale, which play a vital role in driving these
efficiencies.
To meet its expansion goals, DFPL plans to set up a new manufacturing unit exclusively for
fertilizer production, with a planned capacity of 10,00,000 MT per year. The facility will produce
both urea-based and non-urea-based fertilizers, requiring a substantial and consistent supply
of natural gas—a key raw material in fertilizer manufacturing.
Currently, Gaspro India Limited (GIL), through its Paradip unit, is the primary supplier of natural
gas to DFPL’s Brahmapur facility. However, the Brahmapur unit operates on a modest scale,
making it a smaller customer for GIL. Recently, GIL has commissioned a larger Liquefied Natural
Gas (LNG) facility at Gopalpur, Odisha, which has the capacity to meet the significant natural
gas demands of DFPL’s proposed fertilizer unit.
Leveraging  its strong business relationship with GIL and the cost benefits achievable
through economies of scale, DFPL’s Board of Directors has decided to establish the new


====================== PAGE 37 ======================


2.2                  INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

fertilizer manufacturing unit in Gopalpur, Odisha. GIL’s management has also shown
interest in the initiative, as it represents a significant growth opportunity for their business.
A new company, Crop Yield Private Limited (CYPL), will be established as a joint venture
between DFPL and Greenspan GmbH, a globally reputed fertilizer company headquartered in
Germany. Greenspan GmbH is widely recognized for its advanced fertilizer technologies and
energy-efficient manufacturing processes. This collaboration brings together DFPL’s deep
understanding of the southern Indian market and its growing presence in the eastern market
through the Brahmapur unit, making CYPL, DFPL’s largest initiative in the fertilizer sector.
For Greenspan GmbH, this venture marks its entry into the Indian market, an important step
in its global expansion.
Greenspan GmbH will provide technical assistance to CYPL, facilitating the adoption of
modern, energy-efficient manufacturing techniques. Additionally, its subsidiary owns state-
of-the-art machinery with cutting-edge technology and superior processing capacity—far
beyond what DFPL could independently build or acquire within India. This machinery, valued
at ` 120 crores,  will account  for approximately 40%  of the  total plant and equipment
requirements for CYPL’s new facility. By leveraging this advanced technology, CYPL aims to
achieve the production scale necessary to capitalize on cost efficiencies through economies
of scale.
Negotiations and project planning are currently at an advanced stage. Recognizing DFPL’s
limited experience in negotiating joint venture agreements, both parties have engaged Pro
International  (PI), a leading consulting firm, to assist in finalizing the agreement. PI’s
responsibilities include guiding the joint venture’s legal and regulatory compliance, facilitating
the establishment of the manufacturing facility at Gopalpur, and providing management
consulting services to ensure smooth operations and strategic alignment.
Mission of CYPL: “Be a good corporate citizen assisting to Indian farmers to improve their
yield, while also generating wealth for our shareholders by following environment and social
friendly business practices.”
Vision of CYPL: “Be the most preferred supplier of fertilizers in India.”
By September 2025, the company had already purchased land worth ` 100 crores in
Gopalpur. During this time, negotiations for a multi-year purchase agreement between
CYPL and Gaspro India Limited for the supply of natural gas commenced. Other raw
materials required for production are readily available in adequate quantities in the open
market. CYPL’s management plans to  initiate discussions with the suppliers of these
materials after operations begin, as the immediate priority is securing natural gas on
favorable terms and conditions.
CYPL has also procured  plant and machinery  for the  unit, which  require constant
maintenance. To address this, the company has entered into short-term contracts, lasting
less than a year, for factory consumables, primarily tools and other maintenance equipment.
These consumables are supplied by small enterprises located in the industrial area of
Gopalpur. Since full-fledged operations have not yet begun as of 2025, CYPL is just one of
many customers served by these suppliers.


====================== PAGE 38 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                    2.3

DFPL’s facility in Brahmapur will be shut down following the establishment of the new facility in
Gopalpur. Workers have been given the option to either relocate to Gopalpur to continue their
employment or accept one month’s severance pay and exit the company.
Concerned by the developments detailed  in mail communication, DFPL’s Chairman,
Mr. Venkata Ravi, and Greenspan GmbH’s Managing Director, Mr. Gerhard Braun, have
approached Mr. Jeevan Sampath, a partner at the consulting firm Pro International (PI). The
following is an excerpt from their meeting:
    “Jeevan, we are worried about the public resistance to our proposed facility at Gopalpur. If we are required
    to shift the facilities as farther away as 600 kilometers from Gopalpur, the entire venture will become
    unviable. Moreover, we would not want our international brand image tarnished by any controversy. At the
   same time, we are keen to enter the Indian market. DFPL is a partner we trust and would like to leverage
    their experience to begin our venture in the Indian market. Our shareholders primarily consist of large
    financial institutions, they have shown keen interest in our entry into the Indian market due to the potential
    business opportunity that the market offers. Hence, we would like to maintain their perception of these
   developments on a positive note. They have the power to overturn our decision if the project is found to be
    unviable. It is also important to note that government authorities in Germany would not hesitate to take
    legal action if we are found engaging in practices that compromise public or environmental safety at any
    of our facilities worldwide.”                                          − Mr. Gerhard Braun
   “Thank you, Gerhard, for your continued confidence in us. Jeevan, I share Gerhard’s concerns and agree
    that we must address this issue promptly to prevent a potential public relations crisis. Both Gerhard and I
   want to assure you of our unwavering commitment to implementing best practices to safeguard public
    health and environmental safety. While unforeseen events, such as a pipeline leak, are always a possibility,
   we are prepared to take all necessary measures to minimize these risks and their impact.
    Although government authorities have been cooperative in granting us the license for CYPL, the laws
    regarding social justice, public health, and environmental protection are highly stringent, leaving little room
    for error.
   DFPL’s shareholders are aware of these developments, but there has been no dissenting voice yet. This
   may be because our shareholding pattern is largely fragmented, with most shareholders individually
    holding less than 2% of the total share value. They do not actively participate in every decision made by
   DFPL. However, during our latest earnings calls, shareholders expressed keen interest in the Gopalpur
     facility, expecting it to unlock significant value and generate considerable wealth. There were also inquiries
   about the public resistance to the project reported in the media.
   Meanwhile, the workers’ union in Brahmapur is demanding a higher compensation package for the
    relocation of workers from Brahmapur to Gopalpur. CYPL can benefit from the experience and skills of
   these 112 workers, but meeting their demands will set a precedent. If we offer this package, we will
   need to extend a similar one to newly hired workers from the external market, significantly increasing
    operational costs.
      I am also aware that union heads at our other facilities are closely watching these developments. Currently,
    there is no collaboration between the unions at different locations, but mishandling these demands could
   sow discontent across all unions, leading to a company- wide crisis. This would be disastrous for both our
    operations and our domestic reputation.
   Please guide us on how to manage these developments effectively.”            − Mr. Venkata Ravi
    “In situations like these,  it is first important to assess the power and interest of each stakeholder in
    influencing the issue. This assessment will help us determine the best way to communicate with each
    category of stakeholder. Please allow me a few days to evaluate the situation before I can guide you on
    the next steps forward.”                                           − Mr. Jeevan Sampath


====================== PAGE 39 ======================


2.4                  INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

After several rounds of careful negotiations, an amicable settlement was reached between
the management and the displaced workers at Brahmapur. Consequently, DFPL’s facility
in Brahmapur was officially closed on December 31, 2025. Production at CYPL’s Gopalpur
facility commenced on January 1, 2026. Additionally, the issues related to public resistance
to the establishment and operation of CYPL in Gopalpur were fully resolved by January 31,
2026.
Indirect tax matters for CYPL
Mr. Lalit Shah, the newly appointed finance head of CYPL, joined the company on April 1,
2026. A fresh commerce postgraduate with strong academic credentials but no  prior
experience in tax matters, Mr. Shah has been entrusted with preparing CYPL’s GST return.
Recognizing his limited experience in this area, he has identified the need for expert
guidance to ensure accurate and compliant filing.
Earlier in the week, Mr. Ramakant Bhushan, Managing Director of CYPL, also directed Mr.
Shah to investigate potential recovery claims from Paradip Port Trust regarding a shipment
of potash pilfered in June 2026. This incident has raised concerns about potential financial
losses and recovery options.
While reviewing the GST return for the period ending June 30, 2026, Mr. Shah uncovered
a significant issue related to the GST structure for CYPL’s operations. Fertilizers, the
company’s end product, are taxed at a concessional rate of 5% GST. However, key inputs
like ammonia, used in the production of P&K fertilizers, and packaging materials attract an
18% GST rate. He realizes  that  this  structure could lead  to higher working  capital
requirements and a significant accumulation of input tax credits (ITC), potentially straining
the company’s cash flow. Recognizing the impact, Mr. Shah  is eager to explore the
possibility of claiming refunds for unutilized ITC under GST regulations, aiming to optimize
CYPL’s tax position and ensure financial stability.
To handle these complex matters, Mr. Shah, after consulting with Mr. Bhushan, reached out
to CA. Shrihari for professional advice. In his email, he requested comprehensive guidance
on several matters.
Gas Leak in pipeline of Gaspro India Limited
                        Gaspro India Limited (GIL) is a well-established supplier of
                              natural gas to various industrial and commercial companies.
                                                It recently set up its Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) facility in
                             Gopalpur, Odisha, from which it supplies natural gas to customers
                               via a pipeline network spanning nearly 1,000 kilometers across
                         Odisha and Andhra Pradesh. In January 2026, CYPL and GIL,
                           Gopalpur, entered into a 5-year long- term contract for the
supply of natural gas, covering the period from January 2026 to January 2031. Given the large
scale of CYPL’s operations, it became one of GIL’s largest customers.


====================== PAGE 40 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                    2.5

On September 30, 2026, a massive fire broke out following a gas leak that led to an explosion
in GIL’s underground natural gas pipeline at Arjyapalli, Odisha. The incident tragically
claimed the lives of 15 people and caused significant property damage. As a result, GIL was
forced to halt the supply of natural gas through its pipelines in Odisha to all customers for an
extended period until the safety of operations could be fully ensured. This time was necessary
for GIL’s technical team to repair the pipelines and conduct safety tests across the entire
pipeline network originating from its LNG facility in the region.
The suspension of natural gas supply caused major disruptions for all of GIL’s customers, who
relied heavily on gas for their production needs. CYPL also suffered significant losses due to the
sudden stoppage of the gas supply from GIL. Despite the critical business need for natural gas,
the extraordinary nature of the accident made it impossible to predict when GIL would be able
to resume its supply. Consequently, CYPL canceled its long-term contract with GIL.
To maintain minimal operations, in October 2026, Mr. Ramakant Bhushan, Managing
Director of CYPL, approached Natural Gas Suppliers Limited (NGSL)—a competitor of
GIL—for the procurement of natural gas. NGSL, with facilities in Paradip, Odisha, agreed
to supply natural gas to CYPL.
To secure a stable procurement price, CYPL entered into a 5-year fixed-price contract with
NGSL, spanning from October 2026 to September 2031. Compared to GIL, NGSL’s production
capability was significantly smaller, and the volume of gas NGSL could supply to meet CYPL’s
demands was much lower. Additionally, since the Gopalpur region is  still developing as an
industrial hub, there were no other alternative suppliers willing to deliver natural gas to CYPL’s
Gopalpur facility. Therefore, NGSL became CYPL’s sole supplier from October 2026 onward.
Since CYPL is currently operating at a minimal scale of production,  it has lost the cost
efficiencies it could generate from economies of scale. Most of its cost structure consists of
fixed costs that must be incurred regardless of the actual scale of production. CYPL had to
bear fixed expenses such as loan repayments, rent, and regular minimum maintenance of
production facilities, despite operating at minimal capacity. This increased the per-unit cost
of  production  for CYPL. However, due  to  regulatory  constraints governed by  the
government’s subsidy mechanism and market competition, the company could not pass on
the increased cost of production to customers. As a result, CYPL sold its fertilizers at a loss,
further straining its financial position.
Uncertainty regarding “Going Concern” of CYPL
In April 2027, CYPL’s management is in the process of finalizing the financial statements for the
fiscal year 2026-27. The prolonged aftermath  of the GIL  pipeline explosion has  left the
company in a precarious position, with no clear timeline for GIL’s resumption of natural gas
supplies to Gopalpur. The company has been compelled to scale down operations to the bare
minimum, which has  resulted in the loss of a substantial number of customers and severely
impacted its revenue generation capabilities. Dis-economies of scale took away cost efficiencies
that were originally expected to be garnered from such a large venture.


====================== PAGE 41 ======================


2.6                  INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

Cash flow has turned negative due  to  limited inflows, while outflows continue  for fixed
costs such as interest payments, staff salaries, rentals, plant maintenance and upkeep among
other costs. This financial strain has raised serious concerns about the joint venture between
DFPL and Greenspan GmbH, which is becoming increasingly economically unviable. CYPL’s
management, led by  Mr. Ramakant Bhushan, is uncertain about the company’s ability to
continue operations  in the near  future. The management has   decided  to disclose the
uncertainty regarding the company’s “Going Concern” status in the notes to the financial
statements, signaling a potential risk to the company’s continued operations as well as the
management’s plans to deal with these conditions.
In light of this situation, CA. Bharat Chowdhary was appointed as the statutory auditor for the
financial years 2025-26 and 2026-27. The management informed him of the adverse
business conditions and potential “going concern” issues. Recognizing the gravity of the
situation, the auditor noted the company’s financial instability. On June 15, 2027, CA. Bharat
Chowdhary issued his audit report for financial year 2026-27.
In August 2027, under the leadership of Mr. Ramakant Bhushan, CYPL established a recovery
committee  comprising  key management  personnel  from  operations,  finance, human
resources, and marketing, along with DFPL’s executive directors and representatives from
Greenspan GmbH. To strengthen the committee’s approach, Mr. Bhushan invited Mr. Jeevan
Sampath from Pro International, the consulting firm that played a pivotal role in establishing
CYPL at Gopalpur. Mr. Sampath’s external perspective brought valuable insights  into the
company’s recovery strategies. The discussion concluded with a consensus to formulate
actionable strategies
focused on securing subsidy support, implementing capital infusion, executing a market
repositioning strategy, and conducting a comprehensive financial health assessment. For
further details, please refer to the extracts of the committee minutes.
The performance assessment of Mr. Ramakant Bhushan, Managing Director CYPL
Due to the exceptional nature of the business environment CYPL is operating in, it has been
decided that the performance assessment of Mr. Ramakant Bhushan for the financial years
from 2026-27 to 2028-29  will been based with many metrics  like building supply chain
resilience, taking measures to improve market share, reducing costs, improving sales
among others.
Mr. Ramakant Bhushan reports directly to Mr. Venkata Ravi, Chairman DFPL, and Mr. Gerhard
Braun, Managing Director Greenspan GmbH, as a part of the leadership team overseeing
CYPL’s  operations  and  strategic  direction.  The  organizational  structure  ensures
streamlined decision-making and alignment with the broader goals of DFPL and Greenspan
GmbH.
Resumption of supplies under new contract with GIL, Gopalpur, Contract termination
with Natural Gas Suppliers Limited (NGSL), Paradip
GIL restored its normal supply levels to the market by March 2029. NGSL had been a reliable
supplier of natural gas to CYPL until this point. However, once GIL resumed supplies, the


====================== PAGE 42 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                    2.7

management of CYPL decided to scale back the operations to originally planned levels at the
time of inception of the company. Renewing the contract with GIL was consistent with the
original rationale for establishing CYPL at Gopalpur, based on the cost efficiencies of
operating arising from economies of scale.
It was proposed to terminate the contract with NGSL, Paradip. The final decision to terminate
the contract was made on April 1, 2029. Although the original contract with NGSL was valid
for another 30 months, until September 2031, CYPL chose to exit due to the significant
increase in the volume of natural gas supplies that it could procure from GIL. CYPL agreed to
pay an exit cost of ` 15 crores for terminating the contract on April 1, 2029. Following this,
GIL would become the sole supplier of natural gas to CYPL.
                                Annexure: 1
 Shankar Patra/ Union Leader/ Dakshin Fertilizers and Petrochem Ltd. (DFPL), Brahmapur
 From:     “Union Leader “ <ul_brahmapur@dfpl.com>
 Sent:      3rd November 2025 19:00
 To:       “Ramakant Bhushan” <ramakant@cypl.com>
 Cc:        “Venkata Ravi” <nvenkataravi@dfpl.com>
 Subject:   Relocation Compensation Package for Brahmapur Workers of DFPL.
 Date:      3rd November 2025
 Dear Mr. Ramakant,
 We have received the official intimation regarding the compensation package offered by DFPL to
 the workers transitioning to CYPL, Gopalpur. While we appreciate the 5% salary hike, we
 respectfully reiterate our request for a 15% increase as adequate compensation.
 The move to CYPL presents an opportunity for  skill development and career growth, which we
 recognize and value. Consequently, out of 112 workers, only two have opted out of this transition.
 However, relocating operations to Gopalpur from Brahmapur introduces additional challenges. Our
  families  will  continue  residing  in Brahmapur due  to  its  superior  facilities and  infrastructure,
 necessitating a daily two-hour commute for us. The additional costs and inconvenience involved in
  this commute warrant a more substantial compensation adjustment.
 Registered unions across DFPL’s various  locations, representing a combined membership  of
 5,000, are  closely monitoring how the management  is handling the reabsorption  of displaced
 workers from Brahmapur. Although our group at Brahmapur is comparatively small, our successful
  reintegration under fair terms will set a positive precedent, reinforcing the company’s commitment to
 employee welfare during strategic transitions.
 Our collective experience at the Brahmapur facility has equipped us to contribute effectively to
 CYPL from day one, minimizing the need for additional training. This brings immediate value to
 your operations. We believe that addressing our compensation request will foster goodwill among
 the broader workforce and demonstrate management’s dedication  to balancing corporate
 objectives with employee well-being.
 We respectfully urge you to reconsider and approve our request for a 15% salary increase. We
 look forward to a positive response and are eager to continue contributing to DFPL’s and CYPL’s
 success.
 Thank you for your attention to this matter.
 Regards,
 Shankar Patra

2.8                  INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

                                 Annexure: 2

  Lalit Shah/ Finance Head CYPL/ Crop Yield Private Limited (CYPL), Gopalpur
 From:           “ Lalit Shah” <lalits@cypl.com>
  Sent:        23rd July 2026 19:10
  To:          “CA. Shrihari” <cashrihari@shassociates.com>
