           ANSWERS TO THE CASE STUDY 35

I.    Answers to the Multiple Choice Questions
1.     (c)    Total Cost to be capitalised is ` 16,76,545
           Reason:
           As per Ind AS 38, An intangible asset should be measured initially at cost. The
             cost of a separately acquired intangible asset comprises of: (a) its purchase price,
              including import duties and non-refundable purchase taxes, after deducting trade


====================== PAGE 494 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                 35.5

             discounts and rebates; and (b) any directly attributable cost of preparing the asset
                for its intended use. If payment for an intangible asset is deferred beyond normal
               credit terms, then cost of such Intangible asset is the cash price equivalent. The
              difference between this amount and the total payments is recognized as interest
           expense over the period of credit unless it is capitalised in accordance with Ind
         AS 23, Borrowing Costs.
                                                             (Amount in `)

                    Initial Payment                                                10,00,000
              Deferred Payment (5,00,000/1.1)                                 4,54,545
              Legal fees                                                      72,000
             Consultancy fees for implementation                              1,50,000
               Total Cost to be capitalised                                     16,76,545

2.     (c)    Predatory pricing
           Reason:
             Predatory pricing (loss leading) is the practice of selling a product or service at a
             very low price, intending to drive competitors out of the market or create barriers
               to entry for potential new competitors.
3.     (a)    Taking Long Position in Debt and Short Position in Equity
           Reason:
                Profit from distressed securities can be earned through strategic positions, such
            as taking a long position in the company's debt and a simultaneous short position
                in its equity.
4.     (b)   The amount calculated is incorrect as the amount which can be transferred to
           Pune registration is ` 2 Crores
           Reason:
             Transfer of ITC on obtaining separate registrations for multiple places of business
              within a state/ Union Territory [Rule 41A].
           The registered person (transferor), having separate registrations for multiple
             places of business within a state/ union territory, can transfer the unutilised ITC
              (wholly or partly) lying in his electronic credit ledger to any or all of the newly
              registered place(s) of business in the ratio of the value of assets held by them at
             the time of registration. Here, the ‘value of assets’ means the value of the entire


====================== PAGE 495 ======================


35.6                 INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

             assets of the business irrespective of whether ITC has been availed thereon or
               not.
              Total Assets of business           =   ` 900 Crs
            Assets of Pune Registration         =   ` 72 Crs
       % of input credit to be transferred    =    72/ 900 × 100 = 8%
           Amount of ITC that can be transferred =   ` 25 Crs × 8% =` 2 Crs
5.     (d)   The auditor should report the issue of shrinkages in inventories in 'Key Audit
             matter paragraph' and also state how the matter has been dealt with by audit team
           Reason:
           The auditor should report the issue of shrinkages in inventories in ‘Key Audit
             matter paragraph’ and also state how the matter has been dealt with by audit
            team.
            For determining key audit matters, auditor shall take into account, Significant
               auditor’s judgments relating to areas in the financial statements that involved
               significant management judgment, including accounting estimates that have been
               identified as having high estimation uncertainty.
II.   Answers to the Descriptive Questions
6.    An investor can earn profit from distressed securities by taking long position in Debt and
      Short position in equity.
       Following are the examples of earning arbitrage profit:
          (i)     In case company’s condition improves because of priority, the investor will get his
               interest payment which will be more than dividend on his short position in equity
             shares.
           (ii)       If company’s condition further deteriorates the value of both share and debenture
           goes down. He will make good profit from his short position.
     ALTERNATIVE
     The WMPL will take long position in Debt and Short Position in Equity of XYZ Ltd.
      This strategy will be profitable because  if XYZ Ltd. go bankrupt though both Debt and
       Equity will lose value but since debt has a priority over Equity in repayment it will be a
      safe bet.
      However, if financial position of company improves any loss on account of short position
        in Equity will be compensated by gain in the Debt position.


====================== PAGE 496 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                 35.7

7.    Since value of taxable supply of WMPL for the month of September 2025 exceeds ` 50
       lakh, rule 86B of the CGST Rules, 2017 is applicable to it.
      Thus, as per rule 86B, amount available in the electronic credit ledger will be utilized only
       to the extent of 99% of the output tax liability while discharging such tax liability. Balance
    1% of the output tax liability needs to be discharged from electronic cash ledger.
       In terms of the restriction imposed by rule 86B, WMPL can discharge 99% of its output
       tax liability, i.e. ` 6.1875 crores (99% of ` 6.25 crores) from the amount available in
       electronic credit ledger. However, it has to mandatorily discharge the balance 1% of the
       output tax liability i.e. ` 6,25,000 (1% of ` 6.25 crores) through electronic cash ledger
       only.
     The nature of documents on the basis of which ITC would have been taken by WMPL
       are:
          (i)     Invoice or revised invoice issued by the supplier of goods and/or services
           (ii)    Invoice issued by the recipient receiving goods and/or services from unregistered
              supplier in case of reverse charge, subject to payment of tax
           (iii)    Debit note issued by the supplier
         (iv)     Bill of entry or similar document prescribed under the Customs Act
        (v)   Document issued by input service distributor
8.       (i)    General disclosures w.r.t. intangible assets including acquisition of software
          An entity should disclose the following for each class of intangible assets:
               (a)    whether the useful lives are indefinite or finite and, if finite, the useful lives
                     or the amortisation rates used;
               (b)    the amortisation methods used for intangible assets with finite useful lives;
                (c)    the gross carrying amount and any accumulated amortisation (aggregated
                     with accumulated impairment losses) at the beginning and end of the
                     period;
               (d)    the line item(s) of the statement of profit and loss in which any amortisation
                      of intangible assets is included; and
               (e)   a reconciliation of the carrying amount at the beginning and end of the
                    period showing:
                              (i)     additions, indicating separately those from internal development,
                         those acquired separately, and those acquired through business
                          combinations;


====================== PAGE 497 ======================


35.8                 INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

                                (ii)    assets classified as held for sale or included in a disposal group
                              classified as held for sale in accordance with Ind As 105 and other
                           disposals;
                                  (iii)    increases or decreases during the period resulting from revaluations
                      and from impairment  losses recognized  or reversed  in  other
                        comprehensive income;
                          (iv)   impairment losses recognized in profit or loss during the period;
                       (v)    impairment losses reversed in profit or loss during the period;
                          (vi)   any amortisation recognized during the period;
                            (vii)   net exchange differences arising on the transaction of the financial
                         statements into the presentation currency, and on the translation of
                       a foreign operation into the presentation currency of the entity; and
                             (viii)   other changes in the carrying amount during the period.
          (ii)   Conditions for derecognition of intangible assets
         An intangible asset should be derecognized:
               (a)   on disposal; or
               (b)   when no future economic benefits are expected from its use or disposal.
           The disposal of an intangible asset may occur in a variety of ways (e.g. by sale,
            by entering into a finance lease, or by donation).

APPENDIX: CASE STUDY 1 & 2


          Strategic Insights into the Chemical and Petrochemical (CPC) Industry
Industry Landscape
The Chemical and Petrochemical (CPC) industry holds a pivotal position in an economy as it
provides the basic materials required for the functioning of other industries.  It serves as a
reliable indicator of manufacturing trends and supply chain challenges within an economy. The
CPC industry is vast, encompassing various manufacturing sectors that heavily rely on chemicals.
It provides critical feedstock to numerous industries, making it indispensable. In India, the
chemical industry is highly diversified, covering more than 80,000 commercial products11.
The applications of the CPC industry span multiple sectors. In Agriculture and Nutrition, it
plays a crucial role by contributing to fertilizers and crop protection. In the Pharma sector, it
provides essential raw materials for the production of drugs and healthcare products. The
Building and Construction sector benefits from key components such as adhesives, sealants,
and polymers supplied by the CPC industry. Furthermore, it supports Mining and Infrastructure
through specialized chemicals essential for processing and construction activities. In Personal
Care and Hygiene,   it enables the production  of soaps, cosmetics, and  sanitizers. The
Automotive and Transportation sector depends on CPC for high-performance oils and
greases, while the Paint and Coatings industry relies on raw materials for protective and
decorative finishes. Additionally, the Consumer Goods and Electronics sector benefits from
CPC products like plastics, adhesives, and packaging materials, emphasizing the industry’s
indispensability across diverse domains.
This diversity can be broadly classified2 into −
♦    Bulk Chemicals (Commodity Chemicals): Chemicals produced in large quantities but
       with lower value are considered bulk chemicals. They significantly cater to the
     downstream industries. They are further divided into 3 categories alkali, organic and
       inorganic.
♦    Petro-Chemicals: Also known as petroleum distillates, these chemicals are derived
      from petroleum and natural gas by refining. It includes polymers, and synthetic fibres,
      performance plastics and others.


1 https://aiaiindia.com/chemicals/
2 India’s Booming Chemical and Petrochemical Industry: Understanding Industry Landscape, Institute for
Competitiveness    (https://chemicals.gov.in/sites/default/files/inline-files/Report_Understanding_Industry
_Landscape.pdf )


====================== PAGE 499 ======================


A.2                  INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

♦     Specialty Chemicals: Chemicals with high value but low production volume are
      considered specialty chemicals like surfactants, textile chemicals, paints and coating,
      dyes, agrochemicals and  others. This  is highly researched and development-
       intensive category of chemicals. Specialty Chemicals account for more than 50% of
      the total chemical exports from India.
India’s Chemical and Petrochemical (CPC) industry is worth 220 billion USD and  it is
expected to grow to about 300 billion USD by 20303. India offers a lucrative business
ecosystem with favourable investment policies that have attracted global CPC industry,
converting it into a potential investment region to that plays a pivotal role within the global
supply chain.
Various international and national megatrends, such as shifts in consumer preferences and
income demographics, increasing digitalization, and innovation, are expected to drive the
growth of India’s CPC industry in the coming years.
Supply Chain Dynamics
The supply chain of this industry is a complex and multi-stage process. It involves sourcing
of raw materials, transportation, manufacturing, distribution and disposal of waste products.
Analysing this supply chain is crucial to identify potential bottlenecks, inefficiencies and risks
in the process and suggest ways to improve it.
The main stages of supply chain (a simplified generic version for student’s understanding)
within this industry are as below:

CASE STUDIES                                A.3


Agrochemical Industry (Fertilizers/ Crop Nutrition / Speciality Chemicals)
(a core product in our case studies)
Agrochemical industry is an important segment of the giant chemical and petrochemical (CPC)
industry. Like medicines is to human beings, agrochemicals protect plants from insects, pests,
diseases, weeds among others, all of which lead to destruction of agricultural crops. Given the
rising population, agrochemicals play an important role in ensuring food security. It is a highly
regulated industry with lot of emphasis being placed on product safety.
India is an agrarian economy which needs a significant quantity of crop protection and
nutrition products. Catering to food requirements of a growing population along with a
constraint for availability of arable land has created an insatiable demand for high quality
crop nutrition chemicals that can improve productivity from agricultural activities. India is
emerging as a potential hub for production of agrochemicals,  it is already a significant
exporter of these products. The government places special emphasis on ensuring that
farmers can get supplies in the form of fertilizers, pesticides, insecticides and other crop
nutrition solutions at reasonable rates. For this  it has subsidy schemes like the Nutrient
Based Subsidy (for non-urea products) and separate subsidies for urea-based products. It
also provides grants to companies/ firms to promote research and development in order to
develop less hazardous and eco-friendly products.
Snapshot of Value Chain of a Firm in the Agrochemical Industry
The value chain refers to the series of activities a business performs to create and deliver
a product or service to the market. It includes everything from product conception to final
delivery and after-sales service. From forecast and planning to customer support, each
stage  of  the value  chain  contributes  to  creating  innovative,  sustainable, and  safe
agrochemical solutions. By leveraging advanced technologies, maintaining rigorous quality
standards, and aligning with market trends, agrochemical companies can meet the evolving
demands of farmers while addressing environmental and regulatory challenges.
Forecasting and Planning −
The industry is characterized by high production and sales volumes. Hence planning and
forecasting, in particular demand forecasting  is the bedrock in ensuring seamless operations.
Since these are complex activities, forecasting and planning may take the following into
account:
Grouping Diverse Products: Grouping products based on types, such as fertilizers, pesticides,
and specialty chemicals, allows for more accurate demand forecasting and better inventory

figure/fig1/AS:515248451194880@1499856211140/Apetrochemical-value-chain-with-its-associated-supply-
chain-elements.png)


====================== PAGE 501 ======================


A.4                  INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

management. This will help the company identify high performing, fast moving products as
against slow moving low performing products.
Seasonal Forecasting: The agrochemical industry is heavily influenced by seasons, as
different crops require different treatments at various growth stages. Seasonal forecasting
helps companies prepare for demand spikes, aligning production and distribution with
planting and harvesting cycles.
Order Book Analysis: Analysing past order data helps predict future demand patterns,
identifying trends that can inform production planning and inventory management.  It also
helps improve client management systems.
Trend Analysis: Keeping track of market trends, like increased demand for organic or eco-
friendly agrochemicals, allows companies to adjust their strategies and invest in relevant
R&D. Trend analysis  is  crucial  in a dynamic industry where regulatory changes  or
environmental concerns can shift demand patterns.
R&D Laboratory −
Developing Formulations: Companies make crucial investments in R&D to discover effective,
environmentally sustainable and safe products for crop nutrition and protection. Along with
scientific experiments on chemical compounds, advanced technologies such as data
science, artificial intelligence, predictive analysis among others are used to design compounds,
predict efficacy and toxicity of different innovations. Such technologies can aid in company’s
efforts to reduce development timelines and costs.
Approval and Testing: Field trials, toxicity assessments and environmental impact studies
are undertaken as part of rigorous testing procedures to ensure safety, effectiveness and
environmental impact of formulations before they are released. Regulatory approvals from
authorities (such as the Central Insecticides Board in India) are required to ensure the
product meets both national and international safety standards.
Patenting: Post approval of a formulation, a company patents it to protect its intellectual property
rights. Patents prevent competitors from replicating the product for a specified period, which
incentivizes innovation and allows for a competitive edge in the market.
Material Procurement −
Approved Supplier and Vendor Selection: Quality, reliability, compliance with health and safety
standards and cost-effectiveness are critical criteria while choosing suppliers and vendors.
Reliable suppliers are required to ensure a steady flow of quality raw materials.
Long-Term Purchase Agreements: To secure a steady supply and potentially negotiate
better rates, long-term agreements with suppliers are common. This also helps suppliers
plan their own production cycles, which can be beneficial for both parties.


====================== PAGE 502 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                A.5

Just-in-Time (JIT) Arrangements: JIT procurement enables companies to receive materials as
needed. This helps reduce waste, limit exposure of raw materials to potential degradation and
minimize inventory costs.
Conformance  and  Quality  Control  Inspections: Raw  materials  should  meet  required
specifications. For this, inspections and audit are conducted regularly to reduce the risk of
non-compliant or defective material being used in production.
Material Traceability:  It is important to be able to track the origin and movement of raw
materials through the supply chain. This is crucial for crucial for quality control, regulatory
compliance, and in case of recalls or audits. It ensures that any issue at any stage within the
supply chain can be traced back to its source.
Product Manufacture −
Process Types (Continuous, Batch, or JIT): The type of production will depend on demand,
production capacity, storage facility and cost considerations. Agrochemical companies may
choose continuous (constant production) or batch production (smaller, specific quantities).
Some may employ JIT production to align manufacturing with demand, reducing storage
needs and minimizing waste.
Scalability and Capacity Utilization: In case of fluctuating market demands, the company should
be able to scale up or scale down its production capacity to ensure that production resources
are maximized leading to efficient output and cost management.
Process Automation: Technology enabled automation of business processes  is called
process  automation.  Especially  useful  while  performing  repetitive  tasks, automation
improves precision in mixing, processing, and packaging, reducing the risk of human error
and enhancing efficiency. It also supports scalability, allowing companies to quickly ramp
up production when necessary and can help transform business processes.
Maintenance: Maintaining peak performance of assets and equipment requires scheduled
maintenance at regular intervals. This   is essential for efficient operations, upkeep of
equipment, prevent breakdowns and ensure consistent production quality. They minimize
downtime and improve operational longevity.
Quality Control and Inspection: Due to the hazardous nature of many of  its products,
agrochemical companies are carrying out stringent quality checks, ensuring adherence to
regulatory and company standards. Analytical  testing, such as chemical composition
analysis and stability testing, helps ensure product efficacy and safety.
Inventory Management and Distribution −
Warehouse Management: Receiving, storing, packing and shipping products are the day-to-day
processes in warehouse management. These vital behind the scene activities ensure on-time
delivery to customers. Warehouse management systems should optimize use of warehouse


====================== PAGE 503 ======================


A.6                  INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

space to store maximum inventory,  it should be easy for staff to find the required inventory,
accurate and  efficient order  fulfilment,  coordinating  with  suppliers and  transportation
companies so that materials arrive and orders ship on time. Efficient management of these
activities improves productivity while keeping the costs low.
Storing and Handling Hazardous Goods: Since agrochemicals can be hazardous, specialized
storage  facilities with proper ventilation, temperature control, and  spill containment are
necessary.  Minimizing  accumulation  of  expiring  products  through  better  inventory
management. Staff must also be trained in the safe handling of chemicals to ensure safety
and regulatory compliance.
Inventory Traceability and Expiry: Inventory management systems help track expiration
dates and ensure proper rotation of stock. Traceability ensures any issues with a batch can
be quickly isolated, which is especially crucial in the case of hazardous or sensitive chemicals.
Use barcode, QR code, or RFID technology for accurate and efficient track and trace across the
supply chain. Traceability is also important to combat the grey market where spurious
products are sold.
Packing and Dispatch: Proper packaging is essential to maintain product integrity during
transportation. Packing protocols are followed to protect against damage, leaks, and
contamination. Efficient dispatch processes help reduce lead times, ensuring timely delivery
to customers.
Sales Management −
Managing distribution channels: Distribution channels play an important role in maximising
profits for the company. Strong relationships with distributors, wholesalers and retailers is
needed to motivate them to promote the company’s products. Identifying high performing
distributors and high performing products is essential to understand which products to focus
on and which distributor network to utilize to maximise sales. Engaging distributors by
communicating about product launches and promotional activities is important. In order to
have a wider reach, a company can have multiple channels for distribution such as direct
sales or retail partnerships. It is vital to integrate the different distribution channels to ensure
coordination and collaboration between them.
Sales  order management:  Optimizing warehouse  location, warehouse management
systems, robust inventory management systems, minimising lead times and stock outs, real
time tracking and efficient transportation networks, error free and timely delivery of orders
to customers, efficient complaint management processes are essential to ensure customer
satisfaction, strengthens client relationships and drives business growth. Companies enter into
long term supply contracts with their customers hence it is important  to adhere to the terms
and conditions in the contract. Companies, particularly those manufacturing fertilizers are
covered under government subsidy schemes. The government subsidy is based on the


====================== PAGE 504 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                A.7


sales made by the company and hence accountability and traceability of orders is very
important.
Product Pricing: Pricing strategies are influenced by production costs, market demand,
competitor pricing, and regulated pricing, where applicable. In some cases, the price is
regulated by government policies or industry norms. Companies also consider external
factors such as raw material costs and exchange rates, which are particularly significant in
the agrochemical industry due to its reliance on imported raw materials.
Customer Support and After-Sales Service −
Technical Support: As a unique selling proposition, agrochemical companies often provide
on-ground technical support to help farmers with product application and answer questions
regarding usage, safety, and compatibility with crops. Better understanding about the
products would improve crop yield. This would be a key driver for ensuring food security to
a growing population.
Feedback Mechanisms: Feedback from farmers and distributors helps companies improve
products and identify potential issues. This information is valuable for refining R&D and
manufacturing processes.
Product Recall and Traceability: In case of defective or harmful products, companies must
have efficient recall systems to withdraw products from the market and communicate with
users promptly. Traceability systems ensure companies can quickly  identify affected
batches.
Sustainability and Environmental Impact Initiatives: Many agrochemical companies now
offer post-sales services focused on sustainability, such as recycling programs for used
containers and programs to help farmers reduce chemical usage.
Summary
Each of these steps collectively supports an efficient, resilient, and compliant supply chain
in the agrochemical industry. By integrating strong quality controls, safety protocols, and
responsive customer service  practices, agrochemical companies can not  only meet
regulatory requirements but also drive growth in a competitive, regulation-heavy market.
Strategic Role of Ammonia and Natural Gas
Ammonia plays an essential role within industrial chemicals, fertilizers, and mining chemicals.
Its applications range from being a foundational ingredient in nitrogen-based fertilizers that
drive agricultural productivity to serving as a critical component in the production of various
industrial and mining chemicals. Ammonia’s importance in these sectors stems not only
from its chemical properties but also from the essential products derived from  it, which
support the broader economy by ensuring food security, advancing mining efficiency, and


====================== PAGE 505 ======================


A.8                  INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

driving industrial manufacturing. The production of ammonia itself heavily relies on natural
gas, which serves as both a feedstock and a fuel source in the synthesis process.
Natural gas is primarily converted into hydrogen, which is then combined with nitrogen to produce
ammonia. The dependency on natural gas in ammonia production is a key strategic factor,
as fluctuations in natural gas prices directly affect the cost of ammonia production. Reliable
access  to  natural gas can  therefore  stabilize ammonia  production  costs,  ensuring
operational continuity and allowing for predictable pricing of its ammonia-based products.
For companies engaged in diverse fields such
as industrial chemicals, fertilizers, and mining
chemicals, securing both ammonia and natural
gas supplies is vital. Consistent availability of
these resources helps organisations mitigate
risks associated with supply chain disruptions
and cost volatility. This is especially significant
in  times   of   global   uncertainty,  where
geopolitical factors can disrupt natural gas
supply routes or increase prices dramatically.
Thus, focus on ensuring an uninterrupted supply of ammonia and natural gas enhances its
competitiveness and ability to maintain pricing  stability  for  its products. This strategic
approach reinforces the company’s position in the market, allowing it to continue supporting
its key business sectors effectively, remain  resilient  in  volatile markets, and pursue
sustainable growth.
Managing Supply Chain Disruptions
                                     The  COVID-19  pandemic  and  recent
                                                  geopolitical upheavals have underscored
                                             the critical need for resilience and agility
                                                 within global supply chains. For the CPC
                                                       industry, which consumes large volumes of oil
                                         and  gas,  price  volatility has a  substantial
                                           impact on profitability, often requiring cost
                                              escalations that pass to end users and
                                                contribute  to  inflation across  industries.
                                            This highlights the need for robust supply
                                            chain  management  not  only   for  the
                                          chemical industry but also sectors that rely
                                      on its products. Holistic organizational models
like McKinsey’s 7S framework can be used to diagnose the existing operations of the company
and identify any blind spots within the organization. Strategic decisions can then be made to
change the operations  that can help build supply chains that are resilient to disruptions.


====================== PAGE 506 ======================


CASE STUDIES                                A.9

This framework addresses the critical role of co-ordination, where all elements work together
to build organizational effectiveness.
Structure − Organizations are required to be agile in order to respond rapidly to
disruptions.  This  requires  various  functions  within  the  organization  like  sales,
operations, logistics, finance, and infrastructure to be tightly integrated and not operate
in silos. Well defined authority and responsibility structure will enable effective decision
making that promotes alignment across departments to take coordinated action across
the  organization,  thereby  supporting  effective  supply  chain management. Mergers and
acquisitions are very common within the CPC industry specially when strategic decisions
focus on forward and backward integrations within the supply chain. Therefore, many
companies have subsidiaries and other associates like joint venture partnerships as part of
their group structure. These units should also be included while using the McKinsey 7S
framework.
Systems − Systems can include client management systems, risk management systems
etc. Strong processes and streamlined systems are essential in managing complex
supply chains. Real-time communication and decision-making help companies monitor
and adjust to disruptions as they arise. Efficiencies within the systems are vital for
responding to the unpredictable fluctuations in the market, particularly for industries like
the CPC industry, where a stable supply chain  is essential to mitigating price  volatility.
Implementing such systems also helps companies avoid stockouts and better manage
inflationary cost trends.
Style − Style defines organizational culture, the way the company operates and the rules of
conduct. Leadership style, while they may be hard to diagnose, plays a critical role in
developing the ethos of an organization.
For example, within the CPC industry where mergers and acquisitions are common, the
leadership style can very well define the final outcome of these strategic measures.
While an autocratic leadership style may lead to resentment, a delegative leadership
style that promotes autonomy and flexibility,  if not aided by proper direction from the
leader may also create scenarios where there can be a stalemate while taking critical
decisions.  Transparency and  collaboration  play a  significant  role  in  disruption
management.  Leaders  should be  capable  of  taking  proactive  problem-solving
approaches. Fostering teamwork that inculcates an adaptive, responsive organizational
culture ensures that the entire organization aligns with company goals during times of
supply chain stress.
Leadership style should emphasize open communication, ensuring that strategic decisions
cascade through every level of the company. The leader should ultimately be able to
translate the operations of each of the units to work towards goal congruence within the
organization in order to achieve its vision and mission.


====================== PAGE 507 ======================


A.10                 INTEGRATED BUSINESS SOLUTIONS

Staff − The range of activities within the chemical industry requires employees specialising in
a myriad of expertise. Technical staff require the support of staff capable of handling logistics,
sales, procurement and inventory management, finance among others. Recruitment processes
should enable the selection of the ideal candidate within the company’s workforce. Workforce
capacity management should be commensurate  with  the  scale  of  operations and  the
requirements of the company.
Skills − Employees in addition to their specialized skill should also be trained with focus on
skills relating to strategic decision- making, data analysis, and crisis management. Skills like
these are essential for navigating the complexities of supply chain disruptions. Developing
specialized  skills enables teams  to make informed, proactive decisions,  positioning the
organization to adapt quickly to changing market dynamics. In cases where required skill set
cannot be procured in-house, companies may want to consider outsourcing the process /
work to outside vendors.
Shared Values − Agility, teamwork and customer centric approach have become the guiding
principles of many organizations. These values act as guiding principles, promoting a culture of
adaptability and ensuring  all employees are aligned on a shared vision of resilience and
operational stability. Shared Values foster a collective commitment to maintaining supply chain
integrity, even under pressure.
Taking cognizance of increased awareness and concerns of the hazardous nature of many of
their products, environment and public health safety are values that many companies in the
CPC industry have actively imbibed. This is very important in order to build sustainable business
models in the long run.
Strategy − A robust, proactive strategy allows companies to anticipate and mitigate supply chain
disruptions effectively. In the chemical industry, real-time visibility across the supply chain is
essential for tracking fluctuations in raw material supplies. Leveraging technology to enable
end-to-end transparency allows companies to adjust operations based on market trends, helping
them plan for potential shortages or inflationary trends and adapt to changing customer demands.
A well-defined strategy ensures that the company can pivot quickly in response to supply chain
challenges.
In conclusion, the McKinsey 7S framework elements work in harmony to create a supply chain
that  is  agile, transparent, and equipped  to  withstand  disruptions. By embedding  these
principles, companies in the chemical industry—and across sectors—can effectively navigate
market fluctuations, maintain operational stability, and meet evolving customer needs, ensuring a
strong, adaptive supply chain for the future.
